verses.commentary: God's call requires radical departure. Country, people, household: three concentric circles of identity must be left behind. The destination is unspecified (the land I will show you), requiring faith. Abram must leave security for the unknown. This is the beginning of salvation history.

God's promise has seven elements (I will occurs seven times in verses 2-3). Great nation, blessing, great name: these reverse Babel's attempt to make a name (11:4). God gives what humans tried to grasp. 'You will be a blessing' means Abram becomes a channel of blessing to others.

Protection and universal blessing. Those who align with Abram receive blessing; those who oppose him face curse. The climax: all peoples on earth will be blessed. The nations scattered at Babel will be regathered through Abram's seed. This is the gospel in seed form, fulfilled in Christ (Galatians 3:8).

Simple obedience. No recorded questions or objections. Like Noah, Abram acts in faith. Seventy-five years old, well past prime. Faith is not limited by age. Lot accompanies him, which will later cause complications.
Abram brings his household. The journey from Harran to Canaan is significant, crossing from Mesopotamia into the promised land. They arrive: the first step of faith is completed.

Abram enters Canaan. Shechem is in the central hill country, a significant location. The great tree suggests a sacred site. The note that Canaanites were in the land underscores the problem: the land is occupied. The promise requires displacement.

First theophany in Abram's story. God appears and specifies the promise: this land. The promise is not for Abram personally but for his offspring. Abram builds an altar, marking the place of encounter. Worship follows revelation.

Abram moves south. Bethel (house of God) becomes a significant location. Another altar, another invocation. Abram lives as a sojourner, dwelling in tents, worshiping at altars. He claims the land through worship, not conquest.
Further south into the desert region. Abram is exploring the land, moving through it.
Immediate test. God promises the land, but the land cannot sustain life. Famine drives Abram to Egypt. This foreshadows Israel's later descent into Egypt. Abram's faith is tested.
Abram's plan reveals fear. He notices Sarai's beauty and anticipates danger. This is not a compliment but a strategy.
Abram fears assassination. In the ancient world, a powerful man could kill a husband to take his wife. Abram's fear is not irrational but his response is faithless.

The deception. Technically, Sarai is Abram's half-sister (20:12), but this is a lie by omission. Abram prioritizes self-preservation over honesty and protection of his wife. The plan endangers the promise: if Sarai becomes another man's wife, how can she bear Abram's offspring?
The plan unfolds as expected. Sarai's beauty is noticed.
Escalation. Not just any Egyptian but Pharaoh himself takes Sarai. She enters the royal harem. The situation is critical.
Abram benefits materially. He receives wealth as a bride price. The irony is bitter: Abram profits while his wife is endangered and the promise is threatened.
God intervenes. The LORD protects the promise when Abram fails. Plagues on Pharaoh foreshadow the exodus. God guards Sarai's purity and the future of the promise.
Pharaoh confronts Abram. The pagan king is more righteous than the man of faith. Pharaoh recognizes the wrong. 'What have you done?' echoes God's question to Adam (3:13) and Cain (4:10).
Pharaoh returns Sarai untouched. He acts with integrity. Abram is rebuked by a pagan. The man of promise is exposed as a liar and coward.

Expulsion from Egypt. Abram is escorted out, prefiguring the exodus when Israel will be driven out with plunder. God's plan advances despite human failure. Abram keeps the wealth, an undeserved grace.
